claims.claim_text: A legacy GPU request is an exclusive whole-number extended resource claim, unlike CPU requests which can be fractional.
claims.span_hint: line 34 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: Kubernetes resource-management docs directly state that extended resources cannot be overcommitted and invalid examples include 0.5.

claims.claim_text: Dynamic Resource Allocation is stable in Kubernetes v1.35 and lets workloads request devices through claim-based APIs.
claims.span_hint: line 36 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: The v1.35 DRA docs explicitly mark the feature stable and enabled by default.

claims.claim_text: A device plugin discovers devices, reports health, registers a resource name with kubelet, and participates in allocation.
claims.span_hint: line 44 | section: Foundations of Accelerated Computing in Kubernetes
claims.rationale: The device plugin spec page describes registration, ListAndWatch health reporting, and Allocate behavior.

claims.claim_text: Extended resource names follow a vendor-domain/resource pattern and legacy GPU quantities must be whole numbers.
claims.span_hint: line 70 | section: Resource Semantics and Capacity Handling
claims.rationale: Kubernetes docs describe domain-prefixed extended resources and restrict them to whole-number quantities.

claims.claim_text: When a device is marked unhealthy, kubelet decreases allocatable for scheduling new Pods while capacity does not change.
claims.span_hint: line 104 | section: Resource Semantics and Capacity Handling
claims.rationale: The device plugin docs state this behavior verbatim in the unhealthy-device section.

claims.claim_text: GPU resources are expected in resources.limits; if requests are also set they must equal limits, and a limit is used as the request by default.
claims.span_hint: line 114 | section: Where GPU Requests Fit in Pod Scheduling
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes GPU scheduling task page directly documents these request and limit rules.

claims.claim_text: The canonical kubelet device-plugin registration socket path is /var/lib/kubelet/device-plugins/kubelet.sock.
claims.span_hint: line 154 | section: Device Plugin and Runtime Integration
claims.rationale: The device plugin docs name this hardcoded kubelet socket path.

claims.claim_text: A device plugin deployed as a DaemonSet needs privileged access and a mount of /var/lib/kubelet/device-plugins.
claims.span_hint: line 160 | section: Device Plugin and Runtime Integration
claims.rationale: The deployment section says the canonical directory requires privileged access and must be mounted for DaemonSet-based plugins.

claims.claim_text: DevicePluginCDIDevices became generally available in Kubernetes v1.31.
claims.span_hint: line 194 | section: Device Plugin and Runtime Integration
claims.rationale: The device plugin page includes the alpha, beta, and GA progression for DevicePluginCDIDevices and states GA in v1.31.

claims.claim_text: GPU Operator v26.3 documents an NRI plugin path that requires CDI, supported containerd versions, and is not supported with CRI-O.
claims.span_hint: line 196 | section: Device Plugin and Runtime Integration
claims.rationale: This is a real NVIDIA compatibility statement, but the primary source is on docs.nvidia.com, which is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The NVIDIA GPU Operator automates management of NVIDIA drivers, the device plugin, the container runtime, automatic node labeling, and monitoring components.
claims.span_hint: line 208 | section: Operator-Driven GPU Management
claims.rationale: The upstream GPU Operator README describes those managed NVIDIA software components directly.

claims.claim_text: GPU worker nodes can be identified by the NFD label feature.node.kubernetes.io/pci-10de.present=true, which the Operator uses to target GPU nodes.
claims.span_hint: line 229 | section: Operator-Driven GPU Management table, NFD row
claims.rationale: NVIDIA's installation docs use this exact label to identify GPU worker nodes, but the source host is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: DCGM Exporter exposes GPU metrics in Prometheus format.
claims.span_hint: line 233 | section: Operator-Driven GPU Management table, DCGM Exporter row
claims.rationale: The upstream dcgm-exporter repository describes it as an NVIDIA GPU metrics exporter for Prometheus and shows metric names.

claims.claim_text: GPU Operator uses calendar-style versioning, and v26.3.0's component matrix includes NVIDIA Container Toolkit 1.19.0 and NVIDIA Kubernetes Device Plugin 0.19.0.
claims.span_hint: line 238 | section: Operator-Driven GPU Management
claims.rationale: The platform support page directly states the versioning scheme and component matrix, but docs.nvidia.com is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Dynamic on-node driver compilation depends on matching kernel packages such as kernel headers for the running kernel.
claims.span_hint: line 247 | section: Operator-Driven GPU Management
claims.rationale: NVIDIA documents this kernel-header dependency explicitly, but the relevant source is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A NoSchedule taint repels Pods unless they include a matching toleration.
claims.span_hint: line 257 | section: Advanced Workload Placement
claims.rationale: The taints and tolerations docs directly explain NoSchedule behavior.

claims.claim_text: GPU feature discovery can automatically generate labels such as GPU product, GPU count, driver version, and MIG-related labels.
claims.span_hint: line 282 | section: Advanced Workload Placement
claims.rationale: The upstream GPU feature discovery README documents generated labels including product, count, driver version, and MIG metadata.

claims.claim_text: NVIDIA time-slicing oversubscribes GPUs by advertising replicas, but it does not provide memory isolation and shared workloads remain in the same fault domain.
claims.span_hint: line 355 | section: GPU Sharing, Partitioning, and Telemetry
claims.rationale: The upstream device-plugin docs explicitly describe time-slicing replicas and warn that workloads share memory access and fault domain.

claims.claim_text: MIG partitions supported NVIDIA GPUs into hardware-isolated instances with dedicated compute and memory resources, and MIG Manager requires workloads to be stopped during reconfiguration.
claims.span_hint: line 375 | section: GPU Sharing, Partitioning, and Telemetry
claims.rationale: NVIDIA's MIG docs directly cover isolation semantics and the no-running-workloads requirement, but they are off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: AMD's Kubernetes GPU ecosystem commonly exposes resources as amd.com/gpu through its device plugin and operator.
claims.span_hint: line 417 | section: Alternative Ecosystems and Dynamic Resource Allocation
claims.rationale: AMD's primary operator docs document amd.com/gpu, but instinct.docs.amd.com is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Intel Gaudi's Kubernetes stack exposes habana.ai/gaudi through its device plugin and runtime stack.
claims.span_hint: line 417 | section: Alternative Ecosystems and Dynamic Resource Allocation
claims.rationale: Habana's primary Kubernetes docs document the habana.ai/gaudi resource name and operator components, but docs.habana.ai is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: In Kubernetes v1.35, DRA uses resource.k8s.io/v1 APIs including DeviceClass, ResourceSlice, ResourceClaim, and ResourceClaimTemplate.
claims.span_hint: line 427 | section: Alternative Ecosystems and Dynamic Resource Allocation
claims.rationale: The v1.35 DRA concept page names these API objects and version.

claims.claim_text: DRA lets workloads express device selection through claims and attributes instead of only opaque integer device counts.
claims.span_hint: line 455 | section: Alternative Ecosystems and Dynamic Resource Allocation comparison table
claims.rationale: The DRA docs describe claim-based allocation and device selection based on published attributes.

claims.claim_text: DRA objects alone are not sufficient; a DRA-capable driver must be installed to advertise and prepare devices for workloads.
claims.span_hint: line 507 | section: Alternative Ecosystems and Dynamic Resource Allocation
claims.rationale: The DRA docs explicitly say cluster-level API support is not enough without installing resource drivers for the devices being managed.

claims.claim_text: The open-source nouveau driver can bind to NVIDIA hardware first and prevent the expected NVIDIA driver stack from initializing correctly.
claims.span_hint: line 664 | section: Common Mistakes, Ignoring the nouveau Driver
claims.rationale: NVIDIA troubleshooting docs directly describe nouveau blocking nvidia module initialization, but the primary source host is off allowlist.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Device Plugins
further_reading.why_relevant: Best primary source for kubelet registration, allocation flow, unhealthy-device behavior, and CDI device references.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes v1.35 Dynamic Resource Allocation
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the stable v1.35 DRA model, claim objects, and the driver contract needed for device allocation.

further_reading.title: NVIDIA Kubernetes Device Plugin
further_reading.why_relevant: Most useful upstream reference for time-slicing behavior, sharing caveats, and NVIDIA-specific GPU scheduling mechanics.

further_reading.title: Schedule GPUs in Kubernetes
further_reading.why_relevant: Good operator-focused reference for GPU request semantics, labels, and heterogeneous-node scheduling basics.

notes: No shared citation pool exists yet for on-premises/ai-ml-infrastructure, so section_pool_ref is null and source_ids are empty. I skipped the hypothetical opening scenario, quiz prompts, exercise steps, diagrams, code blocks, and the Sources list itself unless nearby prose made a load-bearing factual claim that should carry an inline citation.
